keyEdge: SuperDay format with BST-heavy DSA, LRU Cache LLD, concurrency + production case study, and HM who deep-dives your current architecture

rounds.label: Online Assessment
rounds.overview: 90-minute HackerRank with 3–4 problems spanning Greedy, Sorting, DP, and Graphs. GS OA is medium-hard — expect at least one LC Hard. Verbal reasoning about trade-offs is expected even in written code comments.
tips: LC 166, 680, 70, 42, 295, 34 are directly confirmed in GS OA/CoderPad — solve all before applying
Always show brute force → optimised progression in comments, even in OA
Use long not int for division problems to avoid overflow
CoderPad screening follows OA — code must compile and run, not just be correct in logic
For greedy problems: state the greedy choice property explicitly — GS rewards verbal reasoning

questions.question: Given a string representing a fraction (e.g. '2/5' or '-2/3'), return its decimal representation. If the decimal part is recurring, enclose the repeating part in parentheses. E.g. 2/3 → '0.(6)', 1/4 → '0.25'.
questions.answer:
Long-division simulation with a HashMap tracking remainder → position in result string. When a remainder repeats, a cycle exists — insert '(' at the stored position and append ')'. Handle negatives first using XOR of signs. Use long throughout to avoid integer overflow on large numerators/denominators.

Key cases:
• remainder = 0 → terminating decimal
• remainder repeats → recurring decimal
• denominator divides numerator evenly → integer result
followUps: How do you handle the case where numerator is Integer.MIN_VALUE?
What is the maximum length of the recurring part?
How would you detect the cycle without a HashMap? (Floyd's)
questions.notes: LC 166. Confirmed in GS OA. The HashMap remainder→position trick is the key insight — most candidates miss it.

questions.question: Given a string s, return true if it can become a palindrome by removing at most one character.
questions.answer:
Two-pointer from both ends. On first mismatch, try two options: skip left character or skip right character. Check if either resulting substring is a palindrome. O(n) time, O(1) space.

Edge cases: single character (always true), all-same characters (always true), empty string (true).
followUps: What if you can remove at most k characters?
What is the time complexity if the string is almost a palindrome?
How would you extend this to Unicode characters?
questions.notes: LC 680. Confirmed in GS OA 2024–2025.

questions.question: Count the number of ways to climb n stairs taking 1 or 2 steps at a time. The interviewer specifically checks whether you recognise this as equivalent to Fibonacci.
questions.answer:
Pattern recognition is the point — state immediately: 'This is equivalent to Fibonacci — dp[n] = dp[n-1] + dp[n-2].'

GS wants to see the progression of thinking:
1. Brute force recursion: O(2^n) time, O(n) stack
2. Memoisation: O(n) time, O(n) space
3. Bottom-up DP: O(n) time, O(n) space
4. Space-optimised: O(n) time, O(1) space — two variables

Never jump straight to the optimal — show the evolution.
followUps: What if you can take 1, 2, or 3 steps?
What if some stairs are broken?
How does this change for very large n (10^18)? (Matrix exponentiation)
questions.notes: Confirmed GS OA. The meta-observation 'this is Fibonacci' is what they're explicitly testing — say it first.

questions.question: Implement a dynamic array (Vector) from scratch with get(i), add(e), remove(i), and size(). State time and space complexity for each operation.
questions.answer:
Internal fixed-size array with capacity tracking. When full, double capacity and copy elements.

Complexities:
• get(i): O(1) — direct index access
• add(e): amortised O(1) — O(n) when resizing, but amortised across n adds
• remove(i): O(n) — must shift elements left
• size(): O(1) — maintain size counter

Key distinctions: size (elements present) vs capacity (array length). Java ArrayList uses 1.5x growth factor; textbook uses 2x.
followUps: Why does Java ArrayList use 1.5x growth factor instead of 2x?
How would you make this thread-safe? (Vector vs Collections.synchronizedList vs CopyOnWriteArrayList)
How does System.arraycopy differ from a manual loop?
questions.notes: Confirmed in GS OA. The amortised analysis for add() is the key concept GS probes.

questions.topic: Find First and Last Position in Sorted Array
questions.question: Given a sorted array of integers and a target, find the first and last position of the target. Return [-1, -1] if not found. Must be O(log n).
questions.answer:
Two binary searches: one for the leftmost occurrence (when found, continue searching left half), one for rightmost (when found, continue searching right half). Write as two clean helper methods.

Edge cases GS tests: empty array, single element, element not present, all elements equal to target.
followUps: What if the array has duplicates but is not fully sorted?
Can you do this in a single binary search pass?
How would you find the count of occurrences using this?
questions.notes: LC 34. Confirmed GS CoderPad screening. Code must compile and run — GS runs test cases.

questions.question: Design a data structure that supports: addNum(int num) — adds a number to the stream, and findMedian() — returns the median of all numbers added so far.
questions.answer:
Two heaps: max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half.

Invariant: maxHeap.size() == minHeap.size() OR maxHeap.size() == minHeap.size() + 1

On add:
1. Push to maxHeap (lower half)
2. Move maxHeap top to minHeap (balance)
3. If minHeap > maxHeap in size, move minHeap top back to maxHeap

Median:
• Equal sizes: (maxHeap.peek() + minHeap.peek()) / 2.0
• maxHeap larger: maxHeap.peek()

Complexity: O(log n) add, O(1) findMedian

Note: GS interviewer was not satisfied with approach alone — write the full code.
followUps: What if all numbers are in range [0, 100]? (Bucket sort / counting array)
What if memory is constrained and you only need approximate median?
How would you handle removal of numbers from the stream?
questions.notes: LC 295. Confirmed GS CoderPad. Interviewer moved to puzzles after candidate only explained — always write the code.

rounds.label: DSA / Coding Round
rounds.overview: GS SuperDay DSA round: 2 problems in 60 min, one medium + one hard, with heavy emphasis on BST and tree problems. Verbal reasoning about approach is rated as highly as code quality — state your thinking before typing.
tips: BST problems are GS's most reported DSA pattern — know LCA, kth smallest, validate BST, BST to sorted list cold
Always state brute force first, then identify the bottleneck, then state optimal — GS explicitly rewards this
For tree problems: ask if it's BST or general binary tree — completely different approaches
Calculator/expression evaluation comes up frequently — have Shunting-Yard ready
Concurrency questions appear in Round 3 alongside DSA — know ThreadPool, BlockingQueue, AtomicInteger

questions.question: Given a BST and two nodes p and q, find their Lowest Common Ancestor. Follow-up: extend to a general Binary Tree (not BST).
questions.answer:
BST LCA: exploit BST property.
• Both p and q < root → LCA is in left subtree
• Both p and q > root → LCA is in right subtree
• Otherwise → root is LCA
O(h) time where h is height. O(1) space iteratively.

General Binary Tree LCA (LC 236): recursive post-order.
• If current node matches p or q → return it
• Recurse left and right
• If both sides return non-null → current node is LCA
• If only one side returns non-null → propagate that up
followUps: What if nodes p or q might not exist in the tree?
What if the tree is very deep — how do you avoid stack overflow?
How would you find LCA of multiple nodes, not just two?
questions.notes: Confirmed directly in GS SuperDay DSA round. BST vs general binary tree is a key distinction GS tests.

questions.question: Given a string, model it as a graph where each character at index i points to the next index based on character value. Detect if this graph has a cycle. Write optimised code.
questions.answer:
Model as linked list cycle detection. Each index i maps to next index (e.g. index = (index + charValue) % n). Use Floyd's Two-Pointer (tortoise and hare): slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2. If fast catches slow → cycle.

O(n) time, O(1) space. Alternative HashSet approach uses O(n) space — GS expects Floyd's as 'optimised'.
followUps: What is the time complexity of Floyd's vs HashSet approach?
How would you find the start of the cycle, not just detect it?
What if the string has Unicode characters?
questions.notes: Confirmed GS SuperDay Round 1. GS explicitly asked for 'optimised code' — Floyd's is the expected answer.

questions.question: Given a string expression like '(2*5+7)', evaluate it and return the integer result. Mid-interview the interviewer added: it can also have nested brackets.
questions.answer:
Two stacks: operand stack and operator stack. Process character by character:
• Digit → parse full number, push to operands
• '(' → push to operators
• ')' → evaluate until matching '(' found
• Operator → pop and evaluate while top of stack has >=  priority (left-to-right associativity), then push

Precedence: * and / = 2, + and - = 1. O(n) time and space.
followUps: How would you handle unary minus e.g. '-(2+3)'?
How would you add exponentiation with right-to-left associativity?
What if operators are user-defined with custom priorities? (inject priority map)
questions.notes: Confirmed GS SuperDay. GS added brackets mid-interview to test adaptability — your design must handle it cleanly.

questions.question: Given a 2D matrix, find the maximum value path travelling only diagonally up-left, diagonally down-left, or right. Follow-up: what if directions change?
questions.answer:
2D DP where dp[i][j] = maximum sum to reach cell (i,j).

Transitions (moving left to right):
• From (i-1, j-1) — diagonal up
• From (i+1, j-1) — diagonal down
• From (i, j-1) — straight right

Fill column by column left to right. Answer = max value in last column.

For direction change follow-up: redefine valid transitions accordingly — the DP framework stays the same.
followUps: What if you can move in all 8 directions?
How do you reconstruct the actual path, not just the sum?
What if cells have negative values?
questions.notes: Confirmed GS SuperDay. Always state the recursion first, then convert to DP — GS explicitly rewards showing the thought process.

questions.question: Given an array of bar heights, compute how much rainwater can be trapped between the bars.
questions.answer:
Three approaches — GS rewards showing all three:

1. Brute force: For each bar, scan left and right for the max height. Water = min(leftMax, rightMax) - height[i]. O(n²) time, O(1) space.

2. DP precomputation: Precompute leftMax[] and rightMax[] arrays. O(n) time, O(n) space.

3. Two-pointer optimal: Process from both ends. Move the pointer on the side with lower max. O(n) time, O(1) space.
followUps: How would you solve this in 3D? (LC 407 — Trapping Rain Water II)
What if bars can have fractional heights?
Can you solve this using a monotonic stack?
questions.notes: LC 42. Confirmed GS CoderPad. GS interviewer was satisfied when candidate showed all three approaches progression.

rounds.label: Low Level Design / Machine Coding
rounds.overview: GS LLD round (SuperDay Round 2) focuses on LRU Cache as the primary LLD question, followed by HLD discussion on DB optimisation. The interviewer digs deep into whatever you say — every claim will be challenged.
tips: Build LRU Cache from scratch with HashMap + doubly linked list — do NOT use Java's LinkedHashMap as a shortcut
Know LinkedHashMap accessOrder=true and removeEldestEntry() — GS specifically asks about its internals
Thread-safe LRU extension: ReentrantReadWriteLock — read lock for get(), write lock for put()
For HLD DB questions: know WAL, batch writes, LSM tree vs B-Tree, CQRS cold
For any design: state trade-offs proactively — GS penalises answers without trade-off discussion

questions.question: Implement an LRU Cache with get(key) and put(key, value) both in O(1). The interviewer dug deep into every design decision and asked about Java's LinkedHashMap internals.
questions.answer:
HashMap + Doubly Linked List:
• HashMap: key → Node reference (O(1) lookup)
• DLL: maintains access order (most recent at head, LRU at tail)
• Dummy head and dummy tail simplify edge cases

get(key): find node via HashMap, detach it, move to head — O(1)
put(key, value): if exists → update + move to head. If new → add at head, evict tail if over capacity — O(1)

GS deep dive: LinkedHashMap with accessOrder=true uses identical mechanism — on access, entry moves to tail of internal linked list. Override removeEldestEntry() to evict when size > capacity.
followUps: How would you make this thread-safe? (ReentrantReadWriteLock)
How does Java's LinkedHashMap implement LRU internally?
How would you implement LFU Cache instead? (Least Frequently Used)
What if you need distributed LRU across multiple servers? (Redis)
questions.notes: Confirmed GS SuperDay Round 2. Interviewer dug deep into every decision. Do NOT use LinkedHashMap shortcut — implement DLL manually.

questions.question: Given a log file of store visits, build a data structure to efficiently query: did at least 2 different customers visit the same store on a given day? Discuss space vs query time trade-offs.
questions.answer:
Map<String, Map<String, Set<String>>> → storeId → date → Set of customerIds

Query: storeVisits.get(storeId).get(date).size() >= 2 → O(1)
Build: O(n) time and space

Trade-off GS asked: if space is constrained?
→ Probabilistic: Bloom filter per (storeId, date) pair to approximate count. Near O(1) space, but false positives possible.

Finance context: same pattern used at Barclays for ATM visit fraud detection — flagging if same card used at 2+ ATMs in same city within 1 hour.
followUps: What if you need top-K stores with most visitors on a given day?
How would you handle this at scale — millions of visits per second?
What if you only need approximate counts? (Count-Min Sketch)
questions.notes: Confirmed GS SuperDay. The Bloom filter trade-off answer clearly differentiated strong candidates.

rounds.label: System Design / HLD
rounds.overview: GS system design spans HLD (DB optimisation, write scaling, CI/CD) and production case studies. Expect to defend every architectural choice — the interviewer will challenge any claim you don't back with reasoning.
tips: Know 6 DB write optimisation techniques: WAL, batch writes, read replicas, sharding, CQRS, LSM vs B-Tree
Frame every system design answer with your Barclays experience and real numbers
For production incident: always Mitigate first, Investigate second — never the reverse
Know trunk-based development and why GS prefers it over feature branching
CQRS is a GS favourite — separate write store from read store, know the trade-offs

questions.topic: Optimising Writes in a Database at Scale
questions.question: How would you optimise writes in a database system at scale? The interviewer asked about replication, archival, scaling, and indexing.
questions.answer:
6 techniques — know all cold:

1. Write-Ahead Logging (WAL): log changes before applying — durability without immediate disk flush
2. Batch writes: buffer and flush in bulk — reduces I/O operations drastically
3. Read replicas: offload reads to replicas so write primary isn't contended
4. Sharding: horizontal partition by key — distribute write load across nodes
5. CQRS: separate write store (normalised, write-optimised) from read store (denormalised, query-optimised)
6. LSM Tree vs B-Tree: Cassandra uses LSM (append-only writes, fast, compaction later) vs PostgreSQL B-Tree (read-optimised). For write-heavy workloads: LSM wins.

Finance context: At Barclays, settlement reconciliation handled 2M write ops per batch window. Kafka → bulk insert with batching reduced write ops by 80%.
questions.pseudoCode:
Write optimisation strategy:
1. WAL → durability + fast writes (log sequential, apply async)
2. Batch writes → Kafka consumer → JDBC batch insert
   PreparedStatement.addBatch() → executeBatch() every N rows
3. Read replicas → @Transactional(readOnly=true) routes to replica
4. Sharding → partition by accountId % N shards
5. CQRS → Command DB (PostgreSQL, normalised)
           Query DB (Elasticsearch / Redis, denormalised)
6. LSM Tree → use Cassandra for write-heavy time-series data
              use PostgreSQL for read-heavy relational data
followUps: What are the trade-offs of CQRS? (eventual consistency, dual write complexity)
When would you choose Cassandra over PostgreSQL?
How does WAL relate to crash recovery?
What is the write amplification problem in LSM trees?
questions.notes: Confirmed GS SuperDay Round 2. Always close with your Barclays Kafka batch insert example — real numbers differentiate you.

questions.question: How would you approach a production issue? The interviewer asked about your current system's HLD, possibilities of failure, and your incident response process.
questions.answer:
5-Step Incident Response — GS expects this structured approach:

1. Triage (2 min): Partial or full outage? What's customer impact? Transaction/revenue loss rate?
2. Mitigate first (5 min): Rollback last deployment → toggle feature flag off → redirect traffic to healthy pods. Mitigate BEFORE investigating.
3. Investigate (15 min): APM dashboards (Datadog), Kafka consumer lag, DB connection pool exhaustion, GC pause logs, recent deployment diff
4. Root cause: Correlate spike with deployment time or traffic anomaly. Apply 5 Whys.
5. Post-mortem: Timeline, root cause, action items, monitoring gaps filled

Finance SLA at Barclays: detect in <2 min (alerting), mitigate in <5 min (auto failover to secondary), full restore in <30 min. Runbooks for every known failure mode.
questions.pseudoCode:
Incident response timeline:
T+0:00  Alert fires — PagerDuty
T+0:02  Incident commander declared, bridge opened
T+0:05  Mitigate: rollback last deploy / feature flag off
T+0:15  Investigate: Datadog APM, Kafka lag, DB pool, GC logs
T+0:30  Root cause identified
T+1:00  Full restore confirmed
T+24h   Post-mortem document published
T+1wk   Action items implemented
followUps: How do you decide when to rollback vs hotfix?
What metrics do you monitor to detect issues before customers report them?
How do you design a runbook for a payment processing failure?
questions.notes: Confirmed GS SuperDay Round 3. 'Mitigate first, investigate second' is the key principle GS is testing.

questions.question: Describe your Git branching strategy and CI/CD pipeline design for a real-world financial services project.
questions.answer:
GS prefers trunk-based development: short-lived feature branches (max 1–2 days), merge to main via PR, feature flags for incomplete work. Why: they deploy thousands of times daily — long-lived branches create merge conflicts at scale.

CI/CD pipeline: commit → unit tests → integration tests → SonarQube analysis → Docker build → staging deploy → production (blue-green deployment).

Banking addition: compliance gate before production — OWASP dependency check, SAST scan (Checkmarx/Veracode), Change Advisory Board (CAB) approval window.
questions.pseudoCode:
CI/CD Pipeline stages:
commit
  → unit tests (JUnit, Mockito)
  → integration tests (Testcontainers)
  → SonarQube (coverage gate: >80%)
  → OWASP dependency check
  → SAST scan (Checkmarx)
  → Docker build + push to registry
  → staging deploy
  → smoke tests
  → CAB approval window (banking compliance)
  → production deploy (blue-green)
  → canary traffic shift 5% → 25% → 100%
followUps: What is the difference between blue-green and canary deployment?
How do you handle database schema migrations in CI/CD?
What is a feature flag and when would you use one vs a branch?
questions.notes: Confirmed GS SuperDay Round 2. GS specifically picked a component mid-discussion and asked how you'd design it — be ready to zoom in.

rounds.label: Hiring Manager
rounds.overview: GS HM round is 30–40 minutes covering project architecture deep-dive, behavioural questions, a light coding/design question, and 'Why Goldman Sachs'. The HM specifically probed one candidate's Barclays project — anchor your answers to your banking systems experience.
tips: Prepare your Barclays system deep-dive: what it does, scale numbers, design decisions, and what you'd change today
Read at least one post on engineering.gs.com before the interview — cite it in your 'Why GS' answer
Never say 'great brand', 'better pay', or 'prestigious' — be specific about GS engineering problems
Sprint delay answer: focus on transparency and data-driven communication, not optimism
Self-critical reflection (what I'd redesign today) is the highest signal for GS HMs — growth mindset

questions.question: Walk me through your recent project architecture — pros, cons, how you handle sprint delays, team conflicts, and feature vs deadline prioritisation.
questions.answer:
Framework: 2-minute system description → proactive self-critique.

Describe Barclays system: 'Our payment reconciliation service processes 1M transactions per overnight batch window. It's a Spring Boot microservice consuming from Kafka, writing to PostgreSQL, with a React dashboard for ops. Team of 5.'

Then proactively say what you'd change: '(1) Add Outbox Pattern — we had race conditions between DB commits and Kafka events. (2) CQRS — single DB was a write/read bottleneck during reconciliation + reporting. (3) Distributed tracing from day one — cross-service debugging cost significant man-hours.'

This self-critical reflection is exactly what GS HMs look for.
followUps: What was the biggest technical risk you mitigated in that project?
How did you convince the team to adopt a design decision they disagreed with?
What would you have done differently if you had 2x the time?
questions.notes: GFG report confirms Barclays project was specifically discussed by GS HM. Lead every answer by anchoring to your banking domain.

questions.question: Why Goldman Sachs? (Always asked — GS specifically screens for genuine interest vs generic answers.)
questions.answer:
Be specific, not generic. Three components:

1. Domain fit: 'After 4 years at Barclays building payment/risk systems, I want engineering problems at the scale only GS operates at — trillions in daily trading volume.'

2. Specific team interest: 'I'm interested in Markets Technology / Transaction Banking — the system design challenges of low-latency order execution or real-time risk calculation align directly with what I've been building.'

3. Research signal: 'I read GS's engineering blog post on [cite specific post from engineering.gs.com] — the approach to [specific technical detail] is what I'd want to work on.'

NEVER say: 'great brand', 'better pay', 'prestigious', or generic 'I want to grow'.
followUps: Which GS division/team interests you most and why?
What do you know about how GS's technology stack differs from Barclays?
Where do you see yourself in 3 years at GS?
questions.notes: Read engineering.gs.com before the interview. Citing a specific blog post is a strong differentiator.

questions.question: How do you handle sprint delays? Walk me through a real example.
questions.answer:
GS values transparency and data-driven communication over optimism.

'I track velocity daily. If I see a delay forming 3+ days before sprint end:
1. Identify root cause — scope creep, unexpected complexity, or blocking dependency
2. Inform PM immediately with updated estimate and 3 options: descope non-critical items / negotiate deadline / add short technical spike to de-risk
3. Never surprise stakeholders at sprint end.

Real example: In our payment gateway integration sprint at Barclays, a third-party API had undocumented rate limits we discovered on day 4. I immediately flagged it, proposed descoping the retry logic to a follow-up sprint, and we shipped the core integration on time with a known limitation documented.'
followUps: What if the PM disagrees with your descoping proposal?
How do you prevent the same type of delay from recurring?
How do you balance technical debt vs feature delivery?
questions.notes: GS specifically tests for 'no surprises' communication culture — proactive escalation is the right answer.

questions.question: Design a file management system — entities, APIs, and storage. Follow-up: concurrent delete of a folder while someone uploads inside it.
questions.answer:
Entity model: File(id, name, type, size, ownerId, parentFolderId, createdAt, permissions). Folder is a File with type=FOLDER. Tree structure via parentFolderId self-reference.

APIs:
• POST /files → upload file
• GET /files/{id} → download or get metadata
• PUT /files/{id} → update metadata
• DELETE /files/{id} → delete file or folder (recursive)
• GET /folders/{id}/contents → list children

Concurrent delete follow-up: Soft delete + eventual consistency — mark folder as DELETED, background job cleans up children. Or pessimistic lock on the folder row during deletion (SELECT FOR UPDATE). Optimistic: version field, reject upload if folder version changed during upload.
questions.pseudoCode:
Entity:
File { id, name, type(FILE|FOLDER), size, ownerId,
       parentFolderId FK→File.id, permissions, deletedAt }

APIs:
POST   /files              → upload, create File row
GET    /files/{id}         → stream bytes or metadata
PUT    /files/{id}         → rename, move (update parentFolderId)
DELETE /files/{id}         → soft-delete: set deletedAt timestamp
GET    /folders/{id}/contents → SELECT * WHERE parentFolderId=id AND deletedAt IS NULL

Concurrent delete:
Option A: SELECT FOR UPDATE on folder during upload tx
Option B: Soft delete + async cleanup worker
Option C: Optimistic locking with folder version field
followUps: How would you handle very large file uploads? (Multipart / chunked upload)
How would you implement folder-level permissions?
How would you support file versioning / history?
questions.notes: Confirmed GS HM round. The concurrent delete follow-up is where strong candidates differentiate — soft delete with async cleanup is the cleanest answer.
